title: Offline Ads
summary: Offline ads — TV, radio, print, direct mail, billboards, transit, infomercials — are the channel tech founders dismiss by reflex. That reflex is usually wrong. This chapter covers what offline ads actually are, why their underutilization by the startup world creates cost advantages versus crowded digital auctions, the fit signals that decide whether the channel works for your product, the core tactics (demographic matching, remnant inventory, medium-specific economics), how to test cheaply in local markets with promo codes and unique URLs, and the pitfalls — attribution blindness, demographic mismatch, and launching without the infrastructure to handle inbound response.

concepts.title: What Offline Ads Are and Why Founders Dismiss Them

pretest.prompt: A tech founder hears 'radio advertising' and instinctively rolls their eyes — 'that's what my dad's plumbing business did in 1998.' Before reading on: list the full range of media that sit under 'offline ads,' and articulate the specific reason this reflex is often strategically wrong for a startup. What does the reflex have to do with the price you'd pay on Facebook ads?
pretest.modelAnswer: Offline ads covers a much wider surface than most tech founders picture: broadcast TV and local TV, national and satellite radio, magazines (consumer, trade, local), newspapers, direct mail, billboards, transit ads (buses, taxis, shelters), yellow pages, local print (flyers, directories, church bulletins), and long-form infomercials. Total spend here is still larger than spend on online ads. The reflex is wrong for a specific economic reason: when every venture-backed competitor crowds into the same digital auctions (Google, Facebook, Instagram, TikTok), CPMs in those auctions get bid up by the aggregated demand of thousands of startups chasing the same clicks. Meanwhile, the offline inventory those founders are ignoring goes underpriced — in some cases dramatically so, especially remnant inventory near print deadlines that publishers discount up to ~90% off rate card. Dismissing offline by default isn't clever; it's leaving a pricing dislocation on the table. The discipline is to evaluate offline on the same math you'd apply to SEM or paid social — CPM, reach, attribution, CAC — and let the numbers decide.

lesson.markdown:
## The definition

Offline ads is the traction channel that covers every paid placement living outside the internet: television (national and local spots, plus long-form infomercials), radio (terrestrial and satellite), print (magazines, newspapers, local directories, yellow pages, flyers), direct mail (letters, postcards, catalogs sent through the postal system), billboards (roadside outdoor boards), and transit ads (interior and exterior placements on buses, taxis, benches, and bus shelters). The common thread: you are buying the attention of an audience while they are not on a connected screen browsing your website.

The surface is wider than most founders think, and it scales across budgets. A local teenager can put flyers on telephone poles for $15. A Fortune 100 brand can spend $200 million on a national TV season. A seed-stage startup can buy a single 30-second radio spot in a mid-sized market for $300 and see whether a trackable promo code pulls response. All of these are the same channel.

## The reflex

Ask a room of tech founders about offline ads and you will usually get one of three reactions: confusion (they've never considered it), dismissal (it's for Fortune 500 brands, not startups), or contempt (it's untrackable, last-century, wasteful). The reflex is so strong that offline often gets skipped entirely in the Bullseye brainstorm — which is precisely the kind of blind spot the brainstorm exists to catch.

The reflex has a grain of truth. Offline is harder to attribute than digital. Some formats (billboards, TV branding) genuinely do not produce immediate, measurable direct response. Production costs for a polished TV ad can run tens of thousands of dollars. For a subset of startups — especially early-stage ones with no infrastructure to handle inbound calls — the reflex is a correct conclusion.

But the reflex becomes dangerous when it turns into a default. Used as a default, it filters out an entire class of channels without evaluation.

## Why the reflex is often strategically wrong

Two forces make offline more interesting for startups than the reflex suggests.

**First, underutilization creates pricing dislocations.** Every well-funded startup in your category is bidding against every other well-funded startup in the same Facebook, Google, and TikTok auctions. That aggregate demand lifts CPMs across digital ad inventory. Meanwhile, the offline inventory that tech founders are reflexively skipping is often *underbid* by startups and therefore cheaper per impression than the digital equivalent. Remnant inventory (leftover space publishers are about to lose) can go for 90% off rate card through buying agencies like Manhattan Media or Novus Media. Underutilization is an arbitrage.

**Second, offline has reach profiles digital struggles to match.** Ninety percent of consumers watch TV; the average adult watches around 26 hours per week. Radio still reaches the majority of the country weekly. Newspapers and magazines hit specific demographic slices (older, professional, industry-vertical) with precision digital audiences sometimes fail to replicate. For certain ICPs — a $400M outcome like P90x was driven primarily by infomercials — offline is not a nostalgic also-ran but the primary channel.

## The discipline this chapter is trying to install

Do not dismiss offline ads by reflex. Evaluate them with the same framework you apply to paid digital channels: who is the audience, what does the medium cost per thousand impressions, how will you attribute response, what is the inferred CAC, does that CAC clear your LTV hurdle? If the math works, it is a traction channel like any other. If it doesn't, discard it on the numbers — not on the vibe.

The rest of this chapter teaches the mechanics: how impression economics work, how to match medium to demographic, how to attribute without a pixel, how to test a radio or direct-mail campaign for a few hundred dollars, and the specific ways offline campaigns fail when founders under-prepare.

workedExample.setup: Nikhil is running a 14-person DTC mattress startup, RestWave, selling a $699 foam mattress with 55% gross margin and strong repeat purchase into pillows and sheets. He's spent the last nine months buying Facebook and Instagram ads; blended CAC has crept from $92 to $178 as the auction gets more crowded with competitors. His growth lead pitches testing offline — radio and subway transit. Nikhil's instinct is to say no: 'that's boomer stuff, we're a digital brand.' He agrees to evaluate on the numbers before deciding.
workedExample.walkthrough:
**Step 1 — Compute what Facebook is actually costing now.** RestWave's current paid-social campaign: CPM of $24, click-through 0.8%, CPC = $3.00, landing-page conversion 2.1%. CAC = $3.00 / 0.021 = **$143 ad-spend CAC**, **$178 fully loaded** (agency + creative + tools). LTV over 24 months with repeat purchases and accessories = ~$820. LTV:CAC ≈ 4.6. Still viable, but trending worse each month.

**Step 2 — Pull offline CPMs for comparable reach.** His growth lead gets quotes:
- Local radio (a regional market with ~700,000 listeners weekly, 30-second ad, week-long run): **$1,100**. Implied CPM based on estimated reach of ~1.1M impressions: ~$1.00. Vs. Facebook's $24 CPM.
- Subway transit takeover in two stations: $4,500 for a month. Estimated impressions: ~2.5M. CPM: ~$1.80.
- Full-page ad in a regional weekend newspaper: $2,200. Circulation 180,000. CPM: ~$12.

**Step 3 — Check the reflex against the numbers.** On raw CPM, offline is 2× to 24× cheaper than Facebook impressions. That doesn't automatically mean lower CAC (offline attention is often lower-intent than an Instagram click), but it means the reflex 'offline is wasteful' is assuming a fact the numbers contradict.

**Step 4 — Design a trackable test, not a vibe test.** Nikhil approves a $3,000 radio test in the regional market: four-week run, 30-second spot mentioning 'RestWave.com/calm' as the dedicated URL and 'promo code RADIO50' for $50 off. Both are unique to the radio channel. Every order that uses the code or lands on the URL is attributed to radio; anything else isn't.

**Step 5 — Results.** Over four weeks:
- RADIO50 used on 42 orders at an average order value of $780 (mattress + pillow bundle).
- Direct visits to RestWave.com/calm: 2,900; conversion 1.6% = 46 orders.
- Conservatively de-duplicate overlap, attribute ~60 orders to radio.
- CAC from radio = $3,000 / 60 = **$50**. That's one-third of the fully loaded Facebook CAC.
- Even assuming a 40% attribution-blindness discount (some RADIO50 users would have bought anyway, some direct-URL visits were existing customers), CAC is still ~$83 — still well below Facebook.

**Step 6 — What Nikhil actually learned.** The 'digital brand' reflex would have cost him the single cheapest acquisition channel he has access to at this stage. The discipline was to run the same math he ran on Facebook — CPM, conversion proxy, attributable CAC — on a channel whose sticker he initially wrote off. He now queues up a second market test and a direct-mail test to triangulate. Had the numbers gone the other way (say, CAC of $300), he would have discarded offline on the math and gone back to digital with conviction — which is the point. The framework is 'evaluate, don't reflex.'

contrast.explanation: SEM and offline ads are both paid-acquisition channels, but they sit at opposite ends of the intent spectrum. SEM captures users who have already typed their problem — you're buying active, self-declared intent at a market-clearing auction price. Offline ads buy *interrupted* attention — reach at scale among people not currently searching for your category. That's why SEM's economics revolve around Quality Score and conversion rate measured click-by-click, while offline's economics revolve around CPM, demographic fit, and approximate attribution through promo codes and lift studies. Do not port SEM's measurement discipline one-for-one; offline requires a different measurement playbook because the signal is noisier. But the underlying question — did the CAC clear the LTV hurdle — is identical.

Total spend in offline still exceeds online.

flashcards.back: Because the reflex creates a pricing dislocation. When every well-funded startup crowds into the same Google and Facebook auctions, CPMs there get bid up. Meanwhile, offline inventory the same startups skip is underbid, so CPMs can be significantly lower — sometimes 90% off rate card for remnant inventory. flashcards.front: The offline-ad reflex is dangerous when it becomes a ______ — it filters out an entire class of channels without ______. The right move is to evaluate offline on the same numbers you evaluate SEM or paid social: ______, reach, attribution, and inferred ______.
flashcards.back: default ; evaluation ; CPM ; CAC — offline is a traction channel like any other, and the discipline is to let the math decide, not the vibe.

flashcards.front: Which statement best captures why offline ads remain relevant for startups? (a) They are always cheaper than digital, (b) They offer reach profiles (TV hits 90% of consumers; radio reaches most of the country weekly) that digital audiences can struggle to replicate, and underutilization by startups keeps effective CPMs low for those who actually evaluate them, (c) They have better attribution than digital, (d) They are easier to scale than Facebook.
flashcards.back: (b). Offline is not universally cheaper and has worse attribution. But for specific reach profiles — broad TV audiences, regional radio, precise demographic or industry verticals in trade magazines — it often outperforms digital on CPM, precisely because most startup competitors are ignoring it.

flashcards.front: A founder says 'offline ads are untrackable, so we can't use them.' What's wrong with that sentence, and what's the nuance?
flashcards.back: Offline is harder to attribute than digital, not untrackable. Promo codes, dedicated landing URLs ('restwave.com/radio'), unique phone numbers, 'how did you hear about us?' surveys, and time-correlated lift studies give you approximate but useful attribution. The right framing is 'attribution is noisier, so test-size and measurement discipline matter more' — not 'this channel is unmeasurable and therefore unusable.'

scenarios.setup: A seed-stage founder running a women's-apparel DTC brand says in a team meeting: 'We're a digital-native brand. We don't do offline. Let's put another $20k into Instagram next month.'
scenarios.question: Using the reasoning from this concept, respond to the founder. What should they actually do, and what's the principle?
scenarios.modelAnswer: The sentence 'we're a digital-native brand' is an identity claim, not an analytical one. It tells you nothing about whether offline would work — it tells you only about the founder's self-image. The Bullseye brainstorm explicitly exists to force consideration of every channel, including the ones you'd skip by reflex, and offline is the most commonly reflex-skipped channel among tech founders. The principle: evaluate on CPM and inferred CAC, not on brand narrative. Concrete recommendation: before adding $20k to Instagram, spend one hour pulling CPM quotes on three offline mediums that plausibly match their customer (e.g., a women's lifestyle magazine ad kit, a regional radio spot in a target metro, a direct-mail test to a 3,000-person purchased list). If CPMs are 2-10× cheaper than current Instagram impressions and the demographics match, run a $2-3k trackable test with a unique promo code or landing URL before scaling. If CPMs and attribution math don't clear the bar, discard offline on the numbers and proceed with Instagram confidently. The decision to do or not do offline is fine either way — the decision to skip evaluation isn't.
rubric: Names the reflex/identity-claim failure explicitly
Recommends pulling actual CPM quotes before deciding
Specifies a small, trackable test with a promo code or unique URL
Compares offline CPM to current Instagram CPM as the decision anchor
Leaves room to reject offline on the numbers, not on reflex

scenarios.setup: A growth lead lists the 19 traction channels during a Bullseye brainstorm and says: 'I'll skip offline ads — we're a $5M ARR B2B SaaS, that's obviously not our channel.'
scenarios.question: Is 'obviously' doing real work here? What single offline sub-medium might this team be overlooking, and why?
scenarios.modelAnswer: 'Obviously' is doing no real work — it's a reflex dressed as analysis. For a B2B SaaS, broad TV and consumer radio probably are the wrong sub-mediums. But offline ads is not one thing; it contains trade magazines (niche industry publications with tightly qualified professional readerships), which are often the most precisely-targeted medium a B2B can access outside of LinkedIn. A $5M-ARR B2B selling, say, compliance software to regional banks could find a trade magazine that reaches ~5,000 compliance officers at community banks nationwide. A quarter-page ad with a unique URL might cost $1,500 and be seen by essentially the exact ICP. Compared to LinkedIn ads crowded with every other B2B vendor, a well-chosen trade magazine ad can have a CPM per qualified prospect that LinkedIn can't match. The move: do not lump all offline together. Brainstorm each sub-medium against the ICP and evaluate the ones that survive. 'Trade magazine for our specific vertical' almost always deserves at least one evaluation round for a B2B company serving a defined industry.
rubric: Flags 'obviously' as reflex, not analysis
Distinguishes offline sub-mediums instead of treating offline as monolithic
Identifies trade magazines as the specific high-fit sub-medium for B2B
Gives a concrete cost/reach example that undercuts LinkedIn
Recommends evaluation per sub-medium rather than blanket inclusion or exclusion

teachback.prompt: Teach a fellow founder (a) what 'offline ads' actually includes as a channel, (b) why tech founders dismiss it by reflex and the specific reason that reflex is often strategically wrong, (c) the connection between that reflex and what you pay on Facebook, and (d) the discipline you'd apply to evaluate offline before accepting or rejecting it.

concepts.title: Impression Economics, Attribution, and Remnant Inventory

pretest.prompt: You buy a $7,000 billboard in SoMa for a month and see a 20% spike in signups. A teammate says 'the billboard worked, let's renew.' Before reading on: what's the attribution problem hiding in that conclusion, and name three specific techniques you'd use to separate the billboard's signal from everything else happening in the business that month. What trick lets you buy an offline ad at up to 90% off rate card?
pretest.modelAnswer: The attribution problem: a time-correlated spike doesn't prove causation. Signups might have risen because of a press hit, a seasonal pattern, a product launch, a competitor outage, or random variance. Without isolating the billboard's contribution, you'll renew a campaign that may be doing nothing — or fail to renew one that's doing more than the number suggests. Three techniques to sharpen the signal: (1) dedicated trackable destinations (a unique URL like 'yoursite.com/soma' or a dedicated phone number printed on the billboard, so any traffic through those channels is attributable); (2) promo codes tied to the placement (e.g., code 'SOMA' printed on the board; every order using it attributes to the billboard); (3) geo-lift comparison — look at signups per day from the billboard's geography vs. a matched control geography where the billboard doesn't run, over the same window. The 90%-off trick is remnant advertising: buying leftover ad inventory that publishers are about to lose near their deadlines. Agencies like Manhattan Media or Novus Media specialize in negotiating these discounts on print; remnant inventory also exists in radio, transit, and some TV. It's the single biggest price lever in the channel.

lesson.markdown:
## Two reasons offline economics confuse people

Offline ads have two mechanics that look alien to founders steeped in digital: pricing is driven by reach-based impressions rather than conversion-based auctions, and attribution is indirect rather than pixel-tracked. Both become manageable once you see them clearly.

## Impression economics: CPM and reach, not CPC and intent

Offline ads are priced, almost universally, on how many eyeballs or ears a placement is expected to reach.

- **Billboards** carry a **GRP score** (Gross Ratings Points) based on size, location, visibility, and estimated daily traffic. A full GRP means the board is expected to reach 100% of the driving population in its area across a month. Prices: $700-$2,500/month in secondary markets, $7,000 in a prime metro neighborhood like San Francisco's SoMa, $30,000-$100,000/month in Times Square.
- **Radio** is priced on **CPP** (Cost per Point) — each point represents what it costs to reach 1% of the station's listeners. Typical weekly costs: $500-$1,500 in a local market, $4,000-$8,000 in a major metro like Chicago. Production of the ad itself: $300-$500 for a basic 30-second voice spot, 3-10× for a studio-produced spot with effects.
- **Local TV** spots: roughly $5-$50 per 1,000 viewers for a 30-second slot on one of the 1,300+ local US stations. National airtime averages around $350,000 — out of reach for most startups, but local TV is surprisingly tractable.
- **Print** (magazines, newspapers) is priced per circulation, roughly a CPM structure. The three magazine categories — consumer (millions of readers), trade (industry-specific, hundreds to tens of thousands of highly qualified readers), local (free sidewalk distribution) — have very different CPM profiles. Trade magazines tend to have the best CPM per *qualified* reader for B2B.
- **Direct mail** is priced as a blend of list-purchase cost (~$100 per 1,000 consumer names, more for business) plus production and postage. Bulk-mail rates from the postal service provide meaningful discounts once volume clears a threshold.
- **Transit** (buses, taxis, bus shelters) is priced by placement and duration; interior placements work as direct-response because passengers are a captive audience, while exterior placements function more like billboards.

The common thread: you pay for **reach × time**, not for conversion. Two consequences follow. First, your CAC is bounded above by the arithmetic of `spend / attributed customers`, and the attribution step is the fragile one. Second, price dislocations are common — which is where remnant inventory comes in.

## Remnant inventory: the 90%-off lever

**Remnant advertising** is unsold ad inventory that publishers or broadcasters are about to lose. A magazine going to press tomorrow with a quarter-page of blank space has two options: leave it blank (total loss) or sell it at almost any price. Publishers prefer to sell.

Remnant pricing is routinely 40-90% off rate card. Buying agencies like Manhattan Media and Novus Media specialize in brokering these deals for print — you commit to buy inventory without controlling *which* specific issue or page, and you get a steep discount in exchange. Tim Ferriss's advice: use a remnant agency, then negotiate further using them as a go-between.

Remnant exists beyond print. Radio stations have unsold daypart slots they'd rather fill than leave dead. Transit operators have unsold panels. Even TV networks carry remnant inventory, though less accessibly. If you're insensitive to exact timing and location, remnant is the single largest price lever available in offline ads.

This is also the mechanism that turns underutilization into arbitrage. When startup competitors skip offline, remnant inventory accumulates; when inventory accumulates, publishers discount; when publishers discount, the founder who actually shows up gets a CPM their digital-only competitors can't match.

## Attribution: noisier, but not absent

The digital attribution model — pixel fires, UTM parameters, cohort tracking — does not exist offline. What does exist is a set of practical techniques that produce *approximate* attribution adequate for channel decisions:

- **Unique URLs.** Print `restwave.com/radio` on a radio ad, `restwave.com/subway` on a transit panel. Any traffic to those URLs is attributable — a hard signal. This is the single most common, most reliable offline attribution tool.
- **Promo codes.** Each placement gets its own code (`RADIO50`, `WIRED25`). Redemptions attribute directly. Jason Cohen of Smart Bear used magazine names themselves as promo codes — an ad in *Dr. Dobb's Journal* had a free-book offer redeemable via the code, which gave him per-publication ROI.
- **Dedicated phone numbers.** For phone-driven conversions, a unique 800 number per placement produces clean attribution.
- **'How did you hear about us?' surveys.** A one-question signup field. Jason Cohen used this at Smart Bear to separate the effect of magazine ads, trade shows, and online channels. Self-report is noisy but directionally useful.
- **Time-correlated lift studies.** Measure baseline conversion for two weeks before the ad runs, then measure during and after. The lift is the estimated effect. Weaker than dedicated URLs (can't isolate from other events), but the only tool for brand-style campaigns (billboards, TV) where direct response isn't expected.
- **Geo-matched controls.** Run the ad in market A, don't run it in demographically matched market B, compare signup trajectories. This is the gold standard for mediums where direct response is weak — it isolates the ad's causal effect from temporal confounders.

The discipline: **never launch an offline campaign without the attribution apparatus already in place**. Promo codes printed on the material, landing URLs live on your server, tracking numbers routed to your CRM, baseline metrics logged before launch. Offline campaigns launched 'to see what happens' with no attribution plan produce exactly one output: anecdotes that can't drive decisions.

## When attribution is 'good enough' vs. impossible

Some formats are attribution-friendly: direct mail (coded offer), radio (promo code + URL), infomercials (response phone line and URL — explicitly direct-response by design), trade magazines (coded offer). Some are attribution-hostile by nature: brand-focused billboards, top-of-taxi transit ads, most national TV. For the hostile formats, time-correlated lift and geo-controls are the only tools; accept the signal is coarser and budget accordingly. Rule of thumb: the harder attribution is for a format, the smaller your first test and the higher your threshold for scaling should be.

workedExample.setup: Dana runs Crumbl, a DTC artisan-bread subscription ($42/month, 60% gross margin, 14-month average retention). Blended CAC on Meta has climbed to $110. She wants to test three offline mediums with a $6,000 budget and compare their attributable CACs rigorously. She needs the attribution plan locked in before a dollar is spent.
workedExample.walkthrough:
**Step 1 — LTV and target CAC.** Monthly gross profit = $42 × 60% = $25.20. LTV = 14 × $25.20 = $353. At 3:1 viability, max sustainable CAC = ~$118. Current Meta CAC is $110 — right at the ceiling. She needs a cheaper channel or a new channel to unblock growth.

**Step 2 — Pull quotes across three mediums.**
- **Direct mail** to a purchased list of 5,000 locally-sourced food enthusiasts in her metro: list $500, printing $0.40/piece ($2,000), postage $0.45/piece at bulk rate ($2,250). Total: **$4,750**. Expected response rate for well-targeted direct mail in food/lifestyle: 1-3%.
- **Local radio** — 30-second spot on an NPR-adjacent adult-contemporary station with ~240k weekly listeners. Remnant-style discount through a local agency gets her one-week runs at $850 each. Plans two weeks: **$1,700**.
- **Trade-magazine-style placement** in a regional food-and-wine monthly with circulation 38,000. Quarter-page remnant buy: **$600**.

Budget fits: $4,750 + $1,700 + $600 = $7,050. She scales the direct-mail list to 4,000 pieces instead of 5,000 to land at $5,800 total. Done.

**Step 3 — Set up attribution *before* launch.**
- Direct mail: each envelope carries promo code `BREADMAIL` ($10 off) and URL `crumbl.com/mail`. Handwritten addressing on the outer envelope (raises open rate significantly) and a self-addressed reply envelope for the postal-response variant.
- Radio: dedicated URL `crumbl.com/radio`, promo code `FRESH20` ($20 off first month), mentioned three times per spot.
- Magazine: quarter-page features URL `crumbl.com/fwm`, promo code `FWM15`.
- All URLs live on the site 48 hours before the first impression. All codes configured in the checkout system. Baseline conversion rate logged for the two weeks prior.

**Step 4 — Run and measure over four weeks.**
- **Direct mail (4,000 pieces, $4,000 of the budget):** 78 signups via BREADMAIL code + crumbl.com/mail URL. Response rate: 78 / 4,000 = **1.95%** (in the expected band). **CAC = $4,000 / 78 = $51.30**. Strong — less than half of Meta.
- **Radio ($1,700):** 31 signups via FRESH20 + crumbl.com/radio. **CAC = $1,700 / 31 = $54.80**. Also strong. Note: she also saw a general spike in organic-looking signups during the run weeks vs. baseline (~18 extra), suggesting radio produced a halo beyond tracked signups — but she conservatively ignores this in the CAC number because it can't be isolated from temporal confounders without a geo-control.
- **Magazine ($600):** 4 signups via FWM15 + crumbl.com/fwm. **CAC = $600 / 4 = $150**. Above her LTV:CAC threshold — this placement doesn't work at this size.

**Step 5 — Decision.** Direct mail wins on CAC ($51) and is the most scalable (she can grow from 4,000 to 40,000 pieces with larger list purchases). Radio wins on CPM and adds a brand halo effect she can quantify later with geo-controls. Magazine she kills — not because the category is bad, but because this specific publication at this CAC doesn't clear the bar.

**Step 6 — The remnant lever.** Her next move: contact a print-remnant agency and ask about similar quarter-page placements in comparable regional food magazines at 50-80% off rate card. A placement that cost $600 at the discount she got might be $250-$350 through a remnant agency. At $300, her magazine CAC would be $75 (4 signups × $300 / 4), back inside the viability zone. This is how remnant turns a failing test into a profitable channel without changing the creative.

**The lesson of the numbers.** Dana spent $6,300 total and produced 113 attributable customers at blended CAC = $56. Half of Meta. She also now has a measurement apparatus — URLs, codes, baselines — that will keep working as she scales. The attribution was not a bolt-on; it was a precondition to launching. Had she launched without it, she'd have three anecdotes and no decisions.

contrast.explanation: Tracking tools in Chapter 4 assume a digital substrate: URL parameters, analytics pixels, cohort dashboards. Offline attribution achieves the same *function* — isolating a channel's causal contribution — but through different mechanisms: unique URLs printed on physical media, promo codes redeemable at checkout, dedicated phone numbers, 'how did you hear about us' signup questions, and time-correlated lift studies. The principle is the same (every dollar of marketing must be traceable to some signal) but the tools change. Don't assume offline is untrackable because it lacks pixels; assume it's noisier-trackable and build the apparatus before you launch, not after.

flashcards.front: Define remnant advertising and state roughly how much it can save off rate card.
flashcards.back: Remnant is unsold ad inventory — space a publisher or broadcaster is about to lose because a deadline is imminent or a slot is empty. Publishers prefer a discounted sale to a total loss. Remnant typically goes for 40-90% off rate card. Agencies like Manhattan Media and Novus Media specialize in brokering print remnant deals; similar inventory exists (less accessibly) in radio, transit, and occasionally TV. The cost: you give up control over exact timing and placement.

flashcards.front: Name at least four attribution techniques for offline ads and describe each in one line.
flashcards.back: Unique URLs (print crumbl.com/radio on a radio ad — any traffic is attributable); promo codes (each placement gets a distinct code like WIRED25, redemptions attribute directly); dedicated phone numbers (a unique 800 number per placement routes to your CRM); 'how did you hear about us?' surveys (a signup-form question with channel options, noisy but directional); time-correlated lift studies (measure baseline conversion, then measure during and after the ad runs); geo-matched controls (run in market A, not in matched market B, compare signup trajectories — the gold standard for brand-style campaigns).

flashcards.front: Radio ads are priced on a ______ basis, where each point equals what it costs to reach ______% of the station's listeners. Billboards carry a ______ score that estimates reach of the driving population. Direct mail's cost stack is list (~$______ per 1,000 consumer names) plus printing plus postage, and bulk-mail rates give meaningful discounts once volume clears a threshold.
flashcards.back: CPP (Cost per Point) ; 1 ; GRP (Gross Ratings Points) ; 100 — each medium has its own pricing unit, but all of them reduce to a reach-based CPM once you do the arithmetic, which is what lets you compare them apples-to-apples against digital CPMs.

flashcards.front: You see a signup spike during a billboard month. Which interpretation is most defensible? (a) The billboard caused the spike because the timing lines up, (b) The spike is probably the billboard since nothing else changed, (c) Time-correlation is not causation; without a geo-matched control, dedicated URL, or promo code, you can't isolate the billboard's effect from press, seasonality, product launches, or variance, (d) Offline ads can never be measured, so just renew.
flashcards.back: (c). Spikes are temporally confounded by dozens of other factors. The defensible interpretation requires an isolation mechanism: ideally a geo-control (matched market with no billboard), at minimum a dedicated URL or phone number printed on the board so a portion of the lift is directly attributable. Without one of those, 'the billboard worked' is a story, not a finding.

flashcards.front: State the rule about when to launch an offline campaign.
flashcards.back: Never launch an offline campaign without the attribution apparatus already in place before the first impression runs: unique URLs live on the server, promo codes configured in checkout, dedicated phone numbers routed to the CRM, and baseline conversion metrics logged for the two-to-four weeks prior. A campaign launched 'to see what happens' with no attribution plan produces one output: anecdotes. Anecdotes can't drive scaling decisions.

scenarios.setup: A founder says: 'I'm going to spend $8,000 on a subway takeover in one Manhattan station for a month. If signups go up, we'll expand; if they don't, we'll kill it.'
scenarios.question: What's structurally wrong with the plan as stated? Redesign it so the results are actually decision-grade.
scenarios.modelAnswer: The plan has no attribution apparatus, so any outcome will be uninterpretable. If signups go up, she won't know if the subway caused it or a press hit, a referral loop, or a seasonal tailwind. If signups go down, she won't know if the subway failed or just wasn't enough to offset some other decline. The test will produce an anecdote, not evidence. Redesign: (1) Print a unique URL on every subway panel (brand.com/subway) and a promo code ('NYC20' for $20 off). (2) Log two weeks of baseline conversion and signup data before launch, per source. (3) If possible, choose a matched control — a similar metro where she doesn't run subway ads — and compare trajectories. (4) Set a pre-declared success criterion: attributable CAC (spend / (code redemptions + /subway URL visits that convert)) below some multiple of current blended CAC, say, less than 1.2× current Meta CAC to justify continued spend. (5) Run for four weeks, not one, to reduce weekly variance noise. (6) After the run, compute attributable CAC; compute the lift in total signups vs. baseline and matched market; attribute partial credit for any unexplained lift. Now she has a number, a comparison, and a pre-declared decision rule. The $8,000 now produces a decision instead of an argument.
rubric: Identifies 'no attribution apparatus' as the structural flaw
Prescribes a unique URL and a promo code on the creative
Prescribes baseline logging before launch
Adds a matched-control geography or at least pre-declared success criteria
Extends the test window to reduce variance noise

scenarios.setup: A founder has a $2,000 test budget and wants to buy one quarter-page ad in a national trade magazine whose rate card lists the placement at $9,000. He emails the magazine directly; they won't budge below $7,500.
scenarios.question: What specific move should he make that could get him into that publication for under $2,000? What's the trade-off?
scenarios.modelAnswer: He should approach a print-remnant buying agency (Manhattan Media, Novus Media, or similar) rather than negotiating with the publisher directly. Remnant agencies aggregate demand from publishers with empty inventory near press deadlines, and discounts of 40-90% off rate card are routine. A $9,000 placement could realistically come in at $900-$2,700 through a remnant channel. Tim Ferriss's tactic: use the remnant agency as a go-between, then negotiate lower from that starting point. The trade-offs: (1) he can't pick the specific issue — the agency places him wherever publishers have unsold space, so he loses control over timing; (2) he may not get the premium page placement (front of book, facing contents); (3) production/lead times work on the publisher's schedule. For a quarter-page testing a promo code in a generic-enough issue, none of these trade-offs is a dealbreaker. The only thing he loses is precise control over a variable that probably doesn't move the needle at the test stage. The expected outcome: ~$1,500-$2,500 for the placement, which fits the budget; if the attribution through his promo code and dedicated URL produces a CAC below his threshold, he scales, and only then does he consider paying for specific placements at full rate.
rubric: Identifies remnant advertising / remnant buying agency as the lever
Names the approximate discount range (40-90% off rate card)
Describes the mechanism (unsold inventory near deadline, publisher prefers any sale to total loss)
Names the key trade-off (loss of control over specific issue/placement)
Ties the decision to whether the trade-off matters at the test stage

scenarios.setup: After a direct-mail test (5,000 pieces, $6,000 total cost, 85 redemptions via promo code), a growth lead reports CAC = $70. You notice the team didn't run a baseline conversion measurement in the two weeks prior and didn't geo-match. Your boss asks: 'Is that $70 number trustworthy enough to scale to 50,000 pieces next month?'
scenarios.question: What are the three ways this $70 figure could be misleading, and what would you do before committing to a 10× scale-up?
scenarios.modelAnswer: Three ways the number could be misleading: (1) **Cannibalization of existing customers.** Some of the 85 redemptions may be people who already knew about the brand and would have signed up anyway — they used the promo code because they saw the mailer, but the mailer didn't create them. Without baseline data, you can't net this out. A reasonable discount: assume 15-30% cannibalization. True incremental CAC could be $82-$100, not $70. (2) **Attribution miscount.** If the promo code was usable site-wide (not gated to a mail-specific landing page), existing customers or social-media followers could have used it, inflating attributed count. Check whether any of the 85 redeemers came from an IP block or traffic source that doesn't match 'fresh, mailer-driven visitor.' (3) **Sample-size variance.** 85 conversions out of 5,000 is a ~1.7% response rate. Confidence interval on that at n=5,000 is roughly ±0.35%, meaning the 'true' response rate could be anywhere from 1.4% to 2.1%. At 50,000 pieces, if reality is the low end, actual CAC could be $90. Before scaling 10×: (a) run a 2-week baseline now going forward so future tests have a clean comparison; (b) do a 15,000-piece intermediate step rather than jumping to 50,000 — confirms the response rate is stable at 3× scale before going to 10×; (c) tighten the promo code so it only works for first-time customers, or build a mail-exclusive landing page to harden attribution; (d) stress-test the economics against the pessimistic CAC ($90-$100) — if it still clears the LTV:CAC threshold at that level, the scale-up is defensible; if not, solve attribution and re-test before scaling.
rubric: Names cannibalization / incremental-vs-gross effect as a specific risk
Names attribution leakage (non-mailer recipients using the code) as a specific risk
Names sample-size variance with rough confidence intervals
Prescribes an intermediate-scale step (not 1× to 10× directly)
Prescribes stress-testing economics against the pessimistic CAC before committing

teachback.prompt: Teach a growth engineer (a) how offline ads are priced (reach-based CPM, with medium-specific units like CPP for radio and GRP for billboards), (b) what remnant inventory is and why it produces 40-90% discounts, (c) the practical attribution toolkit for offline — unique URLs, promo codes, dedicated phone numbers, surveys, time-correlated lift, geo-controls — and which tool you use for which format, (d) the rule about attribution apparatus and baselines existing *before* launch.
rubric: Explains reach-based pricing and converts at least two medium units (CPP, GRP) into CPM equivalents
Defines remnant inventory including the mechanism (publisher prefers discounted sale over total loss)
Names at least four attribution tools and maps them to format types (direct-response vs. brand)
States the pre-launch rule: attribution must be set up *before* the first impression
Distinguishes attribution-friendly formats (direct mail, radio, infomercial) from attribution-hostile ones (billboards, national TV branding)

concepts.title: Fit Signals: When Offline Ads Actually Work

pretest.prompt: A bootstrapped B2B SaaS serving compliance officers at community banks is evaluating offline ads. A venture-backed DTC razor company with a $60 LTV is doing the same. A niche enterprise tool selling to astronomy research labs is doing the same. Before reading on: which of these three is most likely to find offline a strong channel, which is most likely to find it unusable, and what's the fit signal that separates them?
pretest.modelAnswer: The B2B SaaS for community-bank compliance officers has strong fit — there are industry trade publications reaching exactly that role at low CPM per qualified reader, which offline-targets an ICP that LinkedIn may cover only with heavy competitive bid-up. The DTC razor company at $60 LTV has marginal fit — the LTV is low relative to the CPM of most offline mediums, so only the cheapest tactics (local radio in targeted metros, direct mail to lookalike lists, trade magazines if there's one for shaving hobbyists) might clear the CAC bar, and any mis-targeting kills the economics fast. The astronomy-lab tool has poor fit — the ICP is so narrow (a few thousand researchers globally) that no mass-reach medium is efficient; offline inventory isn't granular enough to reach them without massive waste. The fit signal: offline works when (a) the target customer is defined by geography or demographic that matches an available medium, (b) the product has enough LTV to amortize reach-based CPMs, or (c) there's a trade/industry publication that already aggregates the exact ICP. It fails when targeting is micro-niche, when LTV is too low to clear CPMs, or when the startup lacks the infrastructure to fulfill response at the scale offline generates.

lesson.markdown:
## The question this concept answers

Given how many sub-mediums offline contains, the operational question is not 'do offline ads work?' — the right framing is 'which of my products and at what stage does offline fit, and which sub-medium within offline matches my specific ICP?' This concept gives you the decision criteria.

## Three strong-fit profiles

**1. Geographic-specific or demographic-specific products.** If your customer base is defined by location or demographic attributes that map cleanly onto an offline medium's audience, offline is often your best channel. Examples:

- A local moving company: direct mail to homeowners in the metro, local radio during commute hours, transit ads in neighborhoods with high rental turnover. Geography *is* the targeting.
- A retirement-planning firm: newspaper classified ads (the demographic still buys newspapers) and direct mail to a list of homeowners aged 55+. The demographic concentration of the medium matches the product's ICP.
- A regional restaurant chain launching in a new city: billboards near exits, local radio, regional magazine placements. Offline dominates because the product is inherently geographic.

For these products, offline is not a challenger channel — it's usually the primary channel, and the tech-founder reflex against it is actively harmful.

**2. Broad consumer products with high enough LTV to amortize CPMs.** TV, radio, and high-volume direct mail require reach-based spend that only pays back when customer LTV is large enough to absorb the CPM cost at low response rates. Examples:

- DTC mattress or furniture ($500-$2,000 AOV, strong repeat): TV and subway takeovers are famously effective. Casper's subway campaigns are the canonical case — a $500+ product in a high-traffic commuter medium. Dollar Shave Club combined a viral YouTube launch with a TV campaign once unit economics were established at scale.
- Financial services, insurance: radio and TV consistently produce customers because LTV over multi-year relationships amortizes the impression cost.
- Infomercial-style products (workout, kitchen, home): long-form TV monetizes at dramatic scale when the product has a compelling demonstration that doesn't fit a 15-second spot. P90x hit $400 million in sales primarily through infomercials.

The fit signal here is the same economic principle as SEM: `LTV / medium CPM` must produce a response rate achievable by the creative. Products with $30 LTV and 1% response rates fail on TV math; products with $800 LTV and the same response rate succeed.

**3. B2B targeting a specific industry with an established trade publication.** Trade magazines are often the most precisely targeted offline medium for B2B, and sometimes the most precisely targeted *any* medium for B2B. A publication like *Aviation Week* reaches decision-makers in aerospace; *Dental Economics* reaches practice owners; *Correctional News* reaches facilities administrators in prisons and jails. These publications aggregate an ICP LinkedIn often covers only with heavy competitive bidding.

Smart Bear Software (Jason Cohen's code-review company) ran ads in developer trade publications like *Dr. Dobb's Journal* with a free-book offer as the promo — a high-quality lead magnet, precisely targeted, measurable through book-order tracking. The CAC math on trade publications for a $500-$5,000 annual contract is often substantially better than LinkedIn ads for the same audience.

## Three weak-fit profiles

**1. Startups without budget to run a minimum-viable test.** Offline's cheapest tests are in the $300-$2,000 range — a local radio spot, a small magazine placement, a 1,000-piece direct-mail drop, a short billboard rental. Below that, you can't buy enough reach to produce a signal above noise. If you literally don't have $1,000-$3,000 to commit to a single test, offline is deferred, not zero. Exception: local flyering, which can genuinely work at $50 (InstaCab hiring cyclists to hand business cards to people hailing taxis), but it's a short-term bootstrap tactic, not a scaling channel.

**2. Startups with micro-niche targeting that no medium can match.** If your ICP is '500 infectious-disease researchers at tier-1 academic hospitals worldwide,' there is no offline medium whose audience maps onto that list without >99% waste. Targeted LinkedIn, direct outreach (Chapter 18), or community building (Chapter 15) will outperform any offline channel for a micro-niche. Offline requires a medium whose audience overlaps meaningfully with yours; if no medium does, offline is the wrong channel.

**3. Startups without the infrastructure to handle inbound response.** Offline ads that work produce response at scale and burstily. A successful radio spot can drive 500 phone calls in an hour. A successful infomercial can produce thousands of orders in an evening. A startup that can't answer the phones, ship the product, process returns, or scale customer support will take a successful offline campaign and turn it into a customer-experience disaster — angry callers, shipping delays, refund loops. This pitfall is covered in detail in the Pitfalls concept, but the fit-check belongs here: **do not run offline ads you aren't operationally ready to service.**

## The decision matrix

Before committing to an offline test, score your situation:

- Does my ICP have a clean geographic or demographic definition? (If yes: strong fit signal.)
- Is there a trade or industry publication whose readership overlaps significantly with my ICP? (If yes: strong fit signal — run it first.)
- Does my LTV support a CPM-based channel where response rates are 0.5-5%? (If yes: a broad-reach medium is economically viable.)
- Do I have $1,000-$3,000 to commit to a single trackable test? (If no: defer until capital is available, use flyering as a bootstrap.)
- Can I operationally handle a 5-10× spike in inbound if the test works? (If no: fix the bottleneck first, then run the test.)

More 'yes' answers means offline deserves a spot in your Bullseye inner ring. Mostly 'no' answers means offline is a later-phase consideration or a wrong-channel match. The point is to evaluate, not to guess.

workedExample.setup: Three founders come to an advisor with the same question: 'should offline ads be in my Bullseye inner ring?' Advisor runs the fit-signal check on each.
workedExample.walkthrough:
**Founder 1 — Maya, regional HVAC services in central Ohio.** ICP: homeowners within 40 miles of Columbus. Product: $200 tune-up, $8,000 furnace replacement, ~$400 average order value across services. LTV over 8 years of recurring service: ~$2,000.

- Geographic definition: extremely clean — Columbus metro.
- Trade publication: n/a — this is consumer.
- LTV vs. CPM: $2,000 LTV against a local-radio CPP of ~$50 in Columbus is strong. 1,000 impressions at ~$1-$3 CPM; even a 0.1% response rate to a $400 service call clears the math.
- Budget: $1,500/month marketing budget — enough for a local radio test.
- Infrastructure: 12-truck fleet, call center handles 200+ calls/day, already operationally ready.

**Fit score: 5/5. Offline is likely her primary channel.** Advisor recommends: local radio and direct mail to ZIP codes with the highest share of homes built in the 1990s (furnace replacement target), budget $2,000/month on direct mail, $1,500/month on radio, unique phone number per medium.

**Founder 2 — Jun, DTC dog supplements, ages 7+.** ICP: dog owners nationally with senior dogs. Product: $35/bottle, 6-month typical subscription, LTV ~$140. Meta CAC is climbing to $55.

- Geographic definition: national, not geographic.
- Trade publication: no obvious one for dog owners (consumer pet magazines exist but are thin).
- LTV vs. CPM: $140 LTV is moderate. At a 1% response rate and a CPM of $12 for a targeted pet magazine, CAC = $1,200 (too high). At a local-radio CPM of $1 in a pet-heavy metro with 3% response from a direct-response promo, CAC is plausible.
- Budget: $8,000/month growth budget — enough for meaningful tests.
- Infrastructure: fulfillment handles 5,000 orders/month, can absorb a 2× spike.

**Fit score: 3/5. Marginal fit — deserves one test, not a primary bet.** Advisor recommends: (a) direct mail to a veterinarian-purchased list of senior-dog owners in 3 metros (the most precisely-targeted offline tactic available to him), (b) pass on radio and TV at this LTV — the numbers are too tight.

**Founder 3 — Ravi, B2B SaaS for correctional-facility food-service managers.** ICP: ~3,500 food-service directors at jails and prisons across the US. Product: $15,000/year. LTV at 4-year retention: $60,000.

- Geographic definition: n/a — national, but role-specific.
- Trade publication: YES — *Correctional News* reaches exactly this readership, plus the American Correctional Association's member publications. This is the strongest fit signal in the entire concept.
- LTV vs. CPM: a $60,000 LTV against a full-page trade magazine ad at $3,000 that reaches 3,500 qualified readers gives him a CPM of ~$860 but a per-qualified-reader cost that crushes LinkedIn's targeting.
- Budget: $15,000/month — ample.
- Infrastructure: sales-led motion, 4-person sales team, can handle inbound demo requests easily.

**Fit score: 5/5 via trade-publication route.** Advisor recommends: trade magazine ads in the two industry publications with a unique landing page (`ravico.com/cn`) and a white-paper lead magnet, sales team routes inbound. This beats LinkedIn on CAC per qualified pipeline because the publication has already filtered the audience to exactly the right role.

**The lesson of the three cases.** 'Does offline work?' is the wrong question. 'Which of offline's sub-mediums matches my specific fit profile, and what does the math say?' is the right one. Maya's fit is geographic; Jun's is marginal and requires the cheapest, most-targeted sub-medium or none at all; Ravi's is trade-vertical and highly specific. Same channel, three different answers — because the answer depends on fit signals, not on the channel category.

contrast.explanation: The three rings of Bullseye rank channels by likelihood of fit; this concept drills into the specific fit signals for one channel so you can decide which ring offline belongs in. A founder using Bullseye may move offline between rings based on how many of these signals apply. The three-rings framework is general; the offline fit signals are channel-specific. They complement each other: Bullseye tells you to consider offline; this concept tells you whether, within offline, there's a sub-medium whose fit profile matches your ICP and stage.

flashcards.front: Name the three strong-fit profiles for offline ads.
flashcards.back: (1) Geographic- or demographic-specific products whose ICP maps cleanly onto a medium's audience (local moving company, retirement planning, regional restaurants). (2) Broad consumer products with LTV high enough to amortize reach-based CPMs (DTC mattresses, financial services, infomercial-style products like P90x). (3) B2B targeting a specific industry with an established trade publication whose readership aggregates the ICP (Smart Bear in *Dr. Dobb's Journal*, any B2B with a strong trade title).

flashcards.front: Name the three weak-fit profiles for offline ads.
flashcards.back: (1) Startups without the ~$1,000-$3,000 minimum budget to run a trackable test (exception: bootstrap-scale flyering can produce early traction). (2) Startups with micro-niche ICPs that no available medium's audience can match without >99% waste. (3) Startups lacking the operational infrastructure (support, fulfillment, phones, shipping) to service a successful offline-driven response burst.

flashcards.front: For B2B, a ______ publication whose readership aggregates your exact ICP is often the most precisely targeted offline medium — and sometimes the most precisely targeted medium of any kind. Smart Bear ran ads in ______ with a ______ offer as the trackable lead magnet.
flashcards.back: trade ; Dr. Dobb's Journal ; free-book (code-review book) — trade magazines are the B2B sub-medium that most reliably beats LinkedIn on cost per qualified impression when the right title exists.

flashcards.front: A DTC skincare brand with $40 LTV and a national consumer ICP is considering a national TV spot. The most accurate assessment is: (a) TV always works for DTC, (b) The $40 LTV is too low to amortize TV's CPMs at realistic response rates; targeted direct mail to demographically-matched lists is more likely to clear the math, (c) Skincare must use TV because that's what big brands do, (d) Offline is a bad fit for any DTC.
flashcards.back: (b). The fit signal is LTV versus medium CPM at achievable response rates. $40 LTV against TV's reach-based pricing fails unless response rates are unusually high (infomercial-style). A smaller-CPM, more precisely-targeted medium like direct mail has a realistic path to clearing the CAC bar; TV does not at this LTV. The right framing is medium-specific, not 'offline yes/no'.

flashcards.front: Why is 'operational readiness to handle response' a fit signal, not just a nice-to-have?
flashcards.back: Because successful offline ads produce response at scale and in bursts. A radio spot can generate 500 calls in an hour; an infomercial can generate thousands of orders in an evening. A startup that can't answer phones, ship orders, or scale support turns a successful campaign into a negative customer-experience event (angry callers, missed shipments, refund spirals). Unserved demand from a working ad is worse than no ad: you've spent the acquisition cost and generated negative word-of-mouth. Therefore: check infrastructure before launching, not after.

scenarios.setup: A solo founder running a $19/month meditation app for professionals asks: 'should I test radio ads?'
scenarios.question: Walk through the fit-signal check and give a yes/no recommendation with reasoning.
scenarios.modelAnswer: Work through each signal. Geographic definition: weak — meditation apps target nationally, not geographically. Trade publication: weak — no meditation trade publication exists for professionals. LTV vs. CPM: LTV at $19/month with ~10-month retention is $190. Radio CPMs are cheap ($1-$3) but response rates to a $19/month signup from radio are typically well under 1% — at a 0.5% conversion from a 100,000-listener ad, that's 500 signups, which at a $1,500 ad cost gives CAC = $3, which is excellent *if* that response rate holds. More realistic response from radio to a subscription signup: 0.05-0.2%. At 0.1%, CAC = $15, still viable. Budget: solo founder — probably has a few thousand dollars. Infrastructure: an app has trivial operational readiness (no physical fulfillment). Score: 2-3/5. Recommendation: **a cautious yes, starting with a single-market local radio test** ($1,500-$2,000 budget) with a dedicated promo code and landing URL, expected to produce ambiguous but not useless data. Better alternatives almost certainly exist (paid social with the same LTV, podcast ads with much better intent match), so offline is probably not the inner ring — but it isn't a reflex-no either. The structured analysis matters more than the final answer; a founder who thinks through the signals gets a better decision than one who either defaults to yes or defaults to no.
rubric: Walks through each of the key fit signals explicitly
Correctly computes an LTV-vs-CPM viability check at realistic response rates
Reaches a defensible recommendation rather than a reflex one
Suggests a close alternative (podcast ads) that might beat offline on the same ICP
Frames the analysis as 'evaluate, don't default'

scenarios.setup: A plumbing-supply distributor selling to independent plumbers nationally wants to add offline to their Bullseye inner ring. Their current channels (SEO for 'wholesale plumbing supplies,' email to captured leads) are producing 80% of customers.
scenarios.question: Which offline sub-medium has the strongest fit for this business, and why? What would you specifically test?
scenarios.modelAnswer: The strongest-fit sub-medium is **trade publications serving independent plumbers and plumbing contractors**. This is a B2B targeting a specific industry where there are well-established trade titles: *PHC News*, *Plumbing Engineer*, *Contractor Magazine*, and regional plumbing-union publications all aggregate exactly this ICP. The readers are decision-makers for wholesale purchasing; the topic is their daily work. Per qualified-reader CPM on these publications is often 5-10× better than LinkedIn ads targeting the same role, because (a) LinkedIn has broad competitive bid-up and (b) trade readers are self-selected into the topic in a way LinkedIn users are not. Specific test: take a quarter-page ad (or a remnant equivalent) in one major trade magazine with a unique landing URL, a lead-magnet offer (e.g., 'The 2025 Plumbing Supply Pricing Guide' PDF), and a dedicated phone number routed to sales. Budget $1,500-$2,500 through a remnant agency, run for 3 months (trade magazines compound over multiple exposures), measure via landing page + phone + promo code. Compare attributable CAC to the SEO/email baseline. If it clears, scale to 2-3 additional publications and negotiate annual contracts for further discounts. Secondary test worth considering later: regional radio in high-contractor-density metros during morning commute dayparts (when plumbers are driving to job sites). Both of these beat, say, a billboard or TV attempt, which would miss the B2B targeting completely.
rubric: Identifies trade magazines as the strongest-fit sub-medium with specific examples
Explains why trade beats LinkedIn on CPM per qualified reader
Specifies a concrete test design (placement, lead magnet, landing URL, phone, budget)
Uses remnant agency as the price lever
Rejects mass-reach offline formats as wrong-fit for this B2B context

teachback.prompt: Teach a fellow founder the fit-signal check for offline ads. Cover: (a) the three strong-fit profiles and why each works, (b) the three weak-fit profiles and why each fails, (c) the role of LTV-versus-CPM arithmetic, (d) the operational-readiness check, and (e) how the answer varies by sub-medium within offline so the right framing is 'which sub-medium fits?' rather than 'does offline work?'
rubric: Names all three strong-fit profiles (geographic/demographic, high-LTV broad consumer, B2B trade)
Names all three weak-fit profiles (no-budget, micro-niche, no-infrastructure)
Explains the LTV-vs-CPM arithmetic as the economic viability check
Includes operational readiness as a fit signal with a consequence example
Frames the decision as sub-medium-specific, not channel-level

concepts.title: Core Tactics: Medium Choice, Demographic Matching, Measurement

pretest.prompt: You've decided offline fits. Your next three decisions are: which medium, which audience inside that medium, and how to measure. Before reading on: (1) what questions would you ask a radio-station ad rep to judge audience fit? (2) Where do you get those answers without having to ask? (3) What do 'dayparts' and 'seasonality' have to do with your CAC?
pretest.modelAnswer: Questions for the ad rep: basic demographics of listeners (age, gender, location), economic demographics (income, occupation), listening patterns by daypart (morning drive vs. afternoon drive vs. evening vs. weekend), and how the listener profile maps to your target customer. You should get most of these answers from the station's **ad kit** (sometimes called media kit, press kit, or audience prospectus) without having to ask anyone — every legitimate publisher and broadcaster produces one on request, and it summarizes circulation/listenership, demographics, and format. Dayparts (the time-of-day segments radio and TV divide their schedule into) and seasonality (the week-of-year and month-of-year patterns in your product's demand) determine CAC because the same ad costs different amounts and produces different response rates at different times. Running a dinner-delivery ad during morning drive produces much worse response than at 4 PM when people are deciding what to eat; running a tax-prep ad in December produces better response than in July. CAC moves with daypart and seasonality by 2-5× in many categories, so getting them right is often the single biggest optimization after medium choice itself.

lesson.markdown:
## Four sequential decisions

Once offline fits, the tactical work is four decisions in sequence: (1) which medium, (2) which audience within that medium, (3) how to measure, (4) when to run. Getting any of these wrong can turn a fit channel into a money drain.

## Decision 1 — Medium choice

The cost and fit profile of each medium inside offline differs sharply. Use this as a starting matrix:

- **Broad reach + brand-building, high budget:** TV (national average $350k airtime + high production cost) or major-metro billboards. Use when LTV supports it and you have a brand story that compresses into 15-30 seconds visually.
- **Broad reach + brand-building, small budget:** local TV spots ($5-$50 per 1,000 viewers on one of 1,300+ US stations), secondary-metro billboards ($700-$2,500/month). Same branding purpose at startup budgets.
- **Broad reach + direct response, moderate budget:** radio (CPP-based, $500-$8,000/week depending on market). Radio is the DTC-friendly broadcast medium because production is cheap and response mechanisms (promo code, URL) are clean.
- **Demographic precision + direct response:** direct mail (~$100/1k names + production + postage). The most targetable offline medium by purchase characteristics.
- **Industry precision:** trade magazines (CPM varies wildly; expect $15-$100 per 1,000 but per-*qualified*-reader cost crushes LinkedIn for the right verticals).
- **Captive-attention direct response:** transit interior ads (bus and subway panels). People in transit are a captive audience for 10-30 minutes.
- **Long-form demonstration + direct response:** infomercials ($50k-$500k production + airtime). Use when the product needs more than 30 seconds to sell (vacuums, workout programs, household products).
- **Local bootstrap:** flyers, community newsletters, yellow pages. Cheap ($50-$500 tests), effective for local products, not scalable beyond the immediate geography.

Pick the medium whose profile matches your product's combination of reach need, targeting granularity, response type, and budget.

## Decision 2 — Demographic matching

Every legitimate publisher, broadcaster, or outdoor company publishes an **ad kit** (also called media kit, media package, press kit, or audience prospectus). Ask for it. Every medium will send it on request. It contains:

- Basic demographics (location, gender, race, age distribution)
- Economic demographics (income, occupation breakdown)
- Content/format profile (what the audience reads or listens to beyond your ad)
- Reach numbers (circulation, listenership, traffic counts)
- Ad spec and pricing

For billboards, the ad kit includes daily drive-by counts and aggregate demographics of the surrounding area. For magazines, it includes circulation, reader demographics, and frequency. For radio, listener profiles by daypart.

**Do not buy an offline placement without reading the ad kit.** The ad kit is how you match medium demographics to your customer demographics — the single most important offline-ads decision after medium choice. If the ad kit reveals the audience skews male 50-65 with rural geography and your product is a women's urban fitness app, you have a mismatch no creative rescue can fix.

The Marketing Publishers Association magazine handbook and similar directories can help you discover magazines whose demographics match your target before you even contact publishers.

## Decision 3 — Measurement

Install the attribution apparatus before launch (covered in the Offline Economics concept). For tactics at the medium level:

- **Print**: unique URL in the ad, unique promo code, QR code if the publication's reader profile uses them.
- **Radio**: unique URL (short, easy to remember — 'yoursite.com/radio'), promo code mentioned 3+ times in the spot, optionally a dedicated phone number.
- **TV**: dedicated URL, promo code on-screen, dedicated phone number (most infomercials use this as the primary response path).
- **Billboard / transit exterior**: harder — a simple URL and a highly memorable code. Accept lower attribution fidelity. Use time-correlated lift or geo-control to supplement.
- **Direct mail**: coded offer, personalized URL (e.g., `crumbl.com/mail-j-smith`), physical reply envelope.
- **Infomercials**: dedicated 800 number *and* URL; infomercial advertisers often **pre-test radio versions** of messaging, calls-to-action, and bonuses to identify what works, then port winning elements into the TV spot. This is a cheap-to-expensive escalation pattern worth copying.

The discipline is the same across mediums: **every placement, every variant, every medium gets its own tracking token**. If the same promo code runs across radio and direct mail, you've blended their attribution and can't separately decide which to scale.

## Decision 4 — Dayparts and seasonality

**Dayparts** are the time-of-day segments radio and TV divide their schedule into. Standard radio dayparts:

- Morning drive (6-10 AM) — highest reach, highest cost, commuting audience
- Midday (10 AM-3 PM) — moderate reach, lower cost, at-home and in-office audience
- Afternoon drive (3-7 PM) — high reach, high cost, evening commuters
- Evening / overnight — cheap, small audience, niche applications

The same ad at the same cost has 3-5× different response rates across dayparts. A local-lunch restaurant ad runs best at 10-11 AM (lunch decision forming) and is wasted during overnight. A home-security product runs best in the evening (when people think about break-ins). Match daypart to the decision moment for your product.

**Seasonality** is the weekly, monthly, and annual cyclicality of your product's demand. Tax prep peaks Jan-Apr; outdoor furniture peaks May-Aug; weight-loss products peak January. Running a campaign against the season doubles or triples CAC; running it with the season halves CAC. Seasonality is often the single biggest calendar lever, and it applies equally to online and offline.

Both dayparts and seasonality are knobs you can turn after your first test has produced baseline data. They're not first-launch decisions — but they're the first optimization pass once a medium is working.

## The specific radio playbook worth stealing

Infomercial marketers have a trick worth generalizing to all offline: **pre-test messaging and offers cheaply on radio before committing to the expensive medium**. A radio spot at $1,000/week can test 3-4 variants of headline, CTA, and bonus. The winner goes into a TV spot where production cost alone is $50k+. This is the same A/B logic applied across mediums, with the cheap medium de-risking the expensive one. Worth copying whenever you're considering a high-production-cost offline placement — test the message cheaply first.

workedExample.setup: Dennis runs a $199/season lawn-care subscription for suburban homeowners in the Nashville metro. He's decided offline fits — geography is clean, LTV is strong ($600 over three seasons on average), he has operational capacity. He has $4,500 for a first-round test and needs to make all four tactical decisions.
workedExample.walkthrough:
**Decision 1 — Medium.** His options:
- Local radio: cheap production, good geographic containment, direct-response-friendly.
- Direct mail: most targetable (homeowners only, specific neighborhoods), measurable.
- Local newspaper / community magazine: demographic match is strong (suburban homeowners read community papers).
- Local TV: possible but production cost eats the test budget.
- Flyers / door hangers: cheapest, very local, trustable for this category.

He picks **two** mediums to compare: direct mail (for precision) and door hangers (for local-bootstrap testing). He skips radio this round because he wants response-rate comparison on two mail-style mediums first.

**Decision 2 — Demographic matching.** For direct mail: he buys a list from a consumer-data vendor filtering on (a) Nashville metro ZIP codes, (b) homeowners (not renters), (c) lot size >0.25 acres (indicates meaningful lawn), (d) household income >$60k. 3,000 names at $100/1,000 consumer-business blend = $300. For door hangers: he targets three neighborhoods where median lot size is largest and median income is in his range; he and two college students will hang 2,000 door hangers across a Saturday and Sunday.

Ad kits consulted: the data vendor's prospectus confirmed ~72% of the list had lawn-care interests in purchase history.

**Decision 3 — Measurement.** 
- Direct mail: coded offer 'MAIL25' for $25 off first season, URL `greenlawn.co/mail`, physical reply card with pre-paid return postage.
- Door hangers: coded offer 'DOOR25' (same dollar discount, different code for channel attribution), URL `greenlawn.co/door`, a tear-off bottom with space for name + phone.

Baseline website conversion rate logged for two weeks prior: 3.1%.

**Decision 4 — Timing.** Nashville lawn care has sharp seasonality: demand builds in March, peaks April-May, tapers through October. He launches the test **in early March** — a week before seasonal demand peaks, when homeowners are starting to think about their lawns but competitors haven't yet filled their calendars. Running the same campaign in November would halve response even with the same list and creative.

**Execution and results over 6 weeks.**
- Direct mail (3,000 pieces, ~$3,500 all-in): 92 signups via MAIL25 + /mail URL. **Response rate: 3.1%**. **CAC = $38**. Clean.
- Door hangers (2,000 pieces, ~$1,000 all-in including cost of student labor): 38 signups via DOOR25 + /door URL. **Response rate: 1.9%**. **CAC = $26**. Cheaper per customer, but lower absolute volume and non-scalable above one metro.

Both cleared his target CAC. Direct mail scales further (he can buy 30,000 names and keep response rate similar); door hangers are a local amplifier with a volume ceiling.

**Next optimizations.**
- He adds a radio test for April (peak season) at morning-drive daypart on the country station (demographic match): 30-second spot, unique URL and promo code. Pre-tests three ad scripts on a small radio-remnant buy first to pick the best script for the full run — the infomercial playbook applied to lawn care.
- He will re-test the same direct mail at a December baseline to quantify the seasonality multiplier (expected 3-4× lower response in off-season; this calibrates future budget cycles).

**The lesson.** Dennis made four decisions, not one. He didn't 'do offline' — he chose direct mail and door hangers for Round 1, matched demographics through a list filter and neighborhood selection, instrumented attribution before launch, and timed the launch to leading-edge seasonality. Each decision affected CAC by 1.5-5×. Defaulting on any of them — using an unfiltered list, running off-season, using a shared promo code across both mediums — would have either blown the test or made the results uninterpretable.

contrast.explanation: Offline economics covers the pricing units (CPM, CPP, GRP) and the attribution toolkit — the *mechanics* of the channel. Core tactics covers the decisions you make *using* those mechanics — which medium, which audience, which measurement token per placement, which daypart, which season. The economics concept tells you what things cost and how to measure; this concept tells you how to sequence the choices that decide whether your test has a chance. You need both: economics without tactics is theory, tactics without economics is guessing.

flashcards.front: What is an ad kit, and why is it the single most important document to get before buying an offline placement?
flashcards.back: An ad kit (also called media kit, media package, press kit, or audience prospectus) is the publisher/broadcaster/outdoor-company document summarizing audience demographics, circulation or listenership, format/content profile, reach metrics, and ad pricing. It's the primary tool for matching medium demographics to your customer demographics — the most consequential decision after medium choice itself. Rule: do not buy an offline placement without reading the ad kit. Every legitimate seller produces one on request.

flashcards.front: Name the four radio dayparts and one rule for matching daypart to product.
flashcards.back: Morning drive (6-10 AM, highest reach, highest cost), midday (10 AM-3 PM, moderate reach, cheaper), afternoon drive (3-7 PM, high reach, high cost), evening/overnight (cheap, small audience). Rule: match daypart to the decision moment for your product — lunch restaurant at 10-11 AM (decision-forming), home security in the evening (peak concern), B2B morning drive (commute-based professional audience). Same ad can have 3-5× different response rates across dayparts.

flashcards.front: The infomercial playbook worth stealing: ______-test your messaging, offers, and calls-to-action on ______ before committing to the expensive medium. A radio buy at ~$______ /week can validate three or four variants at a fraction of the cost of a TV production.
flashcards.back: pre ; radio ; 1,000 — the cheap medium de-risks the expensive one, a general A/B logic applied across mediums whenever production cost is large. Use whenever you're about to commit to a high-production offline placement.

flashcards.front: You run the same $25-off promo code across a radio spot and a direct-mail drop simultaneously, both pointed at the same landing URL. What's the problem?
flashcards.back: Attribution is blended — you can't tell which channel drove each redemption. If results are good, you don't know which to scale; if they're bad, you don't know which to kill. Every placement, every variant, every medium should get its own tracking token (distinct promo code *and* distinct URL). The overhead is trivial; the decision clarity is the point of running the tests in the first place.

flashcards.front: Seasonality can move offline CAC by what rough multiple, and why does it matter operationally?
flashcards.back: 2-5× in most categories (weight-loss in January vs. July, tax prep in April vs. September, outdoor products in summer vs. winter). Operationally: running with the season can halve CAC; running against it can double or triple it. This means a calendar decision — 'launch the campaign the week of March 1 instead of July 15' — can be the single biggest optimization of the round, often larger than creative or targeting tweaks. Budget cycles should respect seasonality, not fight it.

scenarios.setup: A founder of a meal-kit service wants to run a 30-second radio ad in Chicago. She says 'let's just pick morning drive — that's when most people listen, right?'
scenarios.question: Is that the right daypart for her product, and what data would you consult to decide?
scenarios.modelAnswer: Maybe, but 'most people listen then' isn't the deciding criterion — the right daypart is the one that matches the decision moment for her product. For meal kits, the decision window is typically late-afternoon-to-early-evening ('what are we eating tonight?' / 'what are we ordering this week?'), not morning commute. Morning drive reaches the most people but during a moment when they're thinking about getting to work, not dinner. Afternoon drive (3-7 PM) is almost certainly better — people are thinking about dinner and about the week's meal-planning. Evening (7-9 PM) may be even better for the subscription-signup pitch. To decide with data, pull the station's **ad kit** (daypart-by-daypart listener counts and demographics) and ask for any response-rate benchmarks they have from food-category advertisers across dayparts. Second, run a small daypart split test: same ad, same creative, same promo code on different URLs (meal.co/am, meal.co/pm, meal.co/eve) across two weeks. The response rates will tell her empirically, without paying full cost to find out. 'Most people listen' is a reach argument; 'best daypart' is a CAC argument. Match the ad to the decision moment, not to the peak listener count.
rubric: Rejects 'most people listen' as the deciding criterion
Names 'decision moment for the product' as the correct criterion
Suggests ad-kit review as the data source
Prescribes a daypart split test with distinct URLs per slot
Makes the reach-vs-response distinction explicit

scenarios.setup: You're advising a DTC winter-coat brand ($180 LTV, $120 AOV, strong gross margin) that wants to run an offline test *this month*. The month is July.
scenarios.question: Is running this month a good idea? What would you do differently, and why?
scenarios.modelAnswer: Running a winter-coat ad in July is one of the worst possible seasonality choices — demand for the product is at its seasonal floor, so response rates will be depressed by probably 3-5× vs. October-December. A $180 LTV product that needs a reasonable CAC will see CAC balloon in July and look like a failing channel even if the channel is actually strong in-season.
